reference.authors: Breit, Anna; Waltersdorfer, Laura; Ekaputra, Fajar J.; Sabou, Marta; Ekelhart, Andreas; Iana, Andreea; Paulheim, Heiko; Portisch, Jan; Revenko, Artem; ten Teije, Annette; van Harmelen, Frank
reference.title: Combining Machine Learning and Semantic Web: A Systematic Mapping Study
reference.source: ACM Computing Surveys, Volume 55, Issue 14s, Article 313

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors propose a classification system for Semantic Web Machine Learning (SWeML) Systems formalized as an ontology. Key dimensions include: (1) System Pattern Types based on boxology notation describing processing flows between ML and symbolic (SW) modules — Atomic, Fusion, I-Pattern, T-Pattern, Y-Pattern, and Other; (2) ML module characteristics: ML super-categories (Classical ML, Deep Learning, Graph Deep Learning), DL components (Feed Forward, Recurrent, Convolution, Attention), and training types (supervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, reinforcement, unsupervised); (3) SW module characteristics: semantic resource types (thesaurus, taxonomy, ontology, dataset, knowledge base, linked dataset, knowledge graph), degree of semantic exploitation (only labels, simple relations, hierarchical structure, complex structures), and presence of KR/reasoning modules; (4) Task categories (NLP, Graph, Image, Other) with sub-tasks; (5) Application domains; (6) Maturity, transparency, and auditability levels. Processing flows use data structures typed as symbolic (sym) or non-symbolic (data), and algorithmic modules typed as ML or KR (deductive).
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The SWeML boxology and pattern-type classification provide a useful structural framework for describing NeSy architectures, but the taxonomy does not address semantic matching, indicator harmonization, HITL, or multilingual aspects specifically. The SW resource type glossary and the degree-of-semantic-exploitation scale are directly reusable for characterizing ontology usage in the thesis.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The survey identifies 41 distinct processing flow patterns for SWeML Systems classified into six pattern types by structural complexity (Atomic, Fusion, I-Pattern, T-Pattern, Y-Pattern, Other). Over 63% of systems use simple patterns (Atomic or Fusion) with a single ML module. The most common pattern is s-M-s (symbolic input → ML → symbolic output). Deep Learning is the dominant ML super-category (60% of all models), with Plain Encoders (e.g., word2vec), Plain FFNNs, and Translational Distance models as the three most frequent ML categories. Knowledge graphs are the most common SW resource type from 2018 onward. Only 29 out of 476 papers (~6%) combine ML and KR (reasoning) modules, qualifying as NeSy in the strict sense. In those NeSy papers, the ML→KR interaction family dominates (two-thirds), mostly for learning rulesets subsequently refined or applied by a KR module.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The survey positions SWeML Systems in relation to Explainable AI (XAI), noting that SW components can improve interpretability and comprehensibility of ML sub-systems. A related survey by Seeliger et al. (2019) specifically reviewed SW for XAI. Among the 476 papers, only 3 papers report provenance-capturing mechanisms for auditability (Yin et al. 2019 for input contribution tracking, Song et al. 2017 for provenance nodes in RDF triples, Voogd et al. 2019 for expert interaction with model training enabling explainability). HITL is mentioned only in the context of Voogd et al. (2019), where experts provide input values before and after training. Overall, auditability and provenance are identified as weakly covered.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: SWeML Systems are applied across diverse domains: General Domain (>50%), Natural Sciences (26.7%, especially medicine and biology), Culture & Education, Geography & Economics, News & Social Media, Software & Technology, Production of Goods, and Administration & Politics. NLP and Graph tasks are the most frequent (40% and 38.9% respectively). Geographic distribution of research: Asia 43% (China dominant), Europe 29%, North America 19%, with very low representation from Africa, South/Central America, Middle East. Multilingual aspects and specific language coverage are not analyzed. No M&E or indicator harmonization domains are reported.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Only ~6% of SWeML Systems combine ML and KR reasoning modules. System maturity is very low (93.5% low or probably low). Auditability and provenance tracking are almost absent. Terminological inconsistency in reporting SW resource types across communities. Graph Deep Learning models have not yet transitioned from general-domain benchmarks to domain-specific applications. The combination of multiple SW resources is surprisingly unpopular. Information about SW resource type, size, and serialization is weakly reported.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: More uniform reporting using the proposed SWeML ontology. Extension of the boxology with additional algorithmic module types beyond ML and KR, lifecycle phase distinctions, and metadata on resource roles. Deeper analysis of pattern-task correlations. Increased focus on auditability and provenance. Terminology clarification for SW resource types. Transition of Graph DL to domain-specific applications.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The survey confirms that NeSy integration combining ML and KR reasoning is rare (~6%), that HITL and explainability mechanisms are severely underexplored in SWeML Systems, and that auditability/provenance is nearly absent. No work on M&E indicator harmonization, multilingual semantic matching, or heterogeneous indicator alignment is identified. This confirms the thesis gap: applying NeSy approaches with HITL validation to explainable semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators in multilingual M&E contexts is uncharted territory.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This systematic mapping study of 476 SWeML papers provides a comprehensive classification system (boxology patterns, ML/SW component taxonomies, maturity levels) that can serve as a reference framework for characterizing NeSy architectures in the thesis. It empirically confirms the scarcity of true NeSy systems combining ML and KR reasoning, and the near-absence of HITL, explainability, and auditability mechanisms—all core concerns of the thesis—thereby validating the research gap.

reference.authors: DeLong, Lauren Nicole; Fernández Mir, Ramon; Fleuriot, Jacques D.
reference.title: Neurosymbolic AI for Reasoning over Knowledge Graphs: A Survey

section_1_scope.period_covered: Approximately 2015–2024 (no explicit period stated; earliest surveyed work is from 2015, latest from 2024)

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: Three major categories: (1) Logically-informed embedding approaches (symbolic inference augments KG before neural embedding; subcategories: modular two-step, iterative guidance); (2) Learning with logical constraints (constraints on embedding space, constraints on loss function, learning representative logic embeddings); (3) Rule learning for KGC (EM algorithm methods with rule weight learning or iterative rule mining, bridging discrete and continuous spaces for new rules, path-based rule learning). Five cross-cutting characteristics: Interpretability, Guided Training, Underrepresented Types, Heterogeneous Aggregation, Long-Range Dependencies. Mapping to Kautz types: category 1 ≈ Neuro:Symbolic→Neuro / Neuro;Symbolic; category 2 ≈ Neuro[Symbolic] / Neuro_Symbolic; category 3 ≈ Neuro;Symbolic.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The taxonomy is KG-completion-centric and does not directly address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators or multilingual contexts, but its three-way classification of NeSy integration patterns (pre-augmentation, constrained learning, rule learning) and the five cross-cutting characteristics (especially Interpretability, Guided Training, Heterogeneous Aggregation) can be adapted to frame NeSy architectures for indicator harmonization.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant architectures include: (1) sequential pipelines where ontological reasoners or rule engines augment KGs before KGE methods (e.g., Walking RDF and OWL, SoLE, UniKER); (2) constrained embedding approaches where logical rules regularize the embedding space or loss function (e.g., KALE, ComplEx-NNE_AER, SLRE, KeGNN, CQD); (3) rule learning approaches using EM-style iteration between neural KGE and symbolic rule modules (e.g., pLogicNet, ExpressGNN, RNNLogic, Neural LP, DRUM). GNNs (GCN, R-GCN, GAT variants) and classic KGE models (TransE, ComplEx, DistMult) are the most common neural modules. Symbolic modules include ILP/AMIE-style rule mining, OWL reasoners, Markov Logic Networks, fuzzy t-norms, and probabilistic logic.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Interpretability is identified as a key characteristic of NeSy approaches. Rule-learning methods (category 3) are noted as strongest for interpretability because they produce human-readable logical rules with confidence scores (e.g., SAFRAN provides confidence-weighted post-hoc explanations, PoLo generates interpretable metapath-based rules, LNN-MP produces fully interpretable rules). The survey acknowledges that interpretability is ill-defined across the literature and no official metric exists. Embedding-based constraint methods (category 2) are noted as weakest for interpretability. No explicit HITL validation mechanisms are discussed beyond the general notion that rules enable human-level explanations and model tuning.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Domains: biomedicine (drug repurposing, polypharmacy side effects, COVID-19 KG), autonomous driving, social networks, traffic forecasting, recommendation systems, general knowledge benchmarks (YAGO, DBpedia, Freebase, WordNet). Languages: not covered; the survey does not address multilingual settings. Application contexts are primarily KG completion and link prediction tasks.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Underexplored application domains beyond standard benchmarks; lack of multimodal data integration methods; no treatment of conditional/interdependent edge types; limited spatiotemporal reasoning; few-shot learning on KGs not well addressed; ill-defined interpretability with no standard metric; scalability issues with rule grounding; risk of stacking-induced performance gains confounding NeSy benefits; domain knowledge availability limits applicability.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Application to underexplored domains (biomedicine, autonomous driving, finance); multimodal data integration using heterogeneous aggregation; modeling conditional/interdependent edge types via rules; spatiotemporal reasoning on KGs; few-shot learning leveraging rule-based KG augmentation for underrepresented types; end-to-end differentiable rule learning bridging discrete and continuous spaces.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The survey does not address M&E indicator harmonization, multilingual semantic matching, or HITL-driven indicator alignment. However, several identified gaps are directly relevant: (1) the need for domain-specific NeSy applications beyond benchmarks parallels the thesis goal of applying NeSy to M&E contexts; (2) heterogeneous aggregation characteristic maps onto matching heterogeneous indicator schemas; (3) the interpretability discussion supports the thesis need for explainable matching; (4) the guided training characteristic via domain ontologies could inform how M&E ontologies constrain indicator matching. Multilingual aspects are entirely absent from the survey.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This survey provides the primary reference taxonomy for classifying NeSy architectures over knowledge graphs into three integration patterns (logically-informed embeddings, constrained learning, rule learning) with five cross-cutting characteristics. It supplies theoretical framing for how symbolic rules and neural embeddings can be combined for interpretable reasoning on heterogeneous graph structures, directly informing the architectural design space for the thesis's NeSy-based indicator harmonization system.

reference.authors: Pedro Giesteira Cotovio, Ernesto Jimenez-Ruiz, Catia Pesquita
reference.title: What can knowledge graph alignment gain with Neuro-Symbolic learning approaches?

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not specified explicitly; references span from 2007 to 2023

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors adopt Kautz (2022) six-type taxonomy of NeSy integration (Type I: Symbolic Neurosymbolic / standard deep learning; Type II: Symbolic[Neuro] / loosely coupled hybrid with neural core in symbolic solver; Type III: Neuro|Symbolic / neural and symbolic handling complementary tasks; Type IV: Neuro:Symbolic→Neuro / symbolic constraints on neural training; Type V: Neuro_{Symbolic} / symbolic rules mapped to embeddings as loss regularizers, e.g. LTN; Type VI: Neuro[Symbolic] / fully integrated symbolic engine in neural network). They cross-reference these types with five KGA challenges: Tackling Large Size, Handling Heterogeneity, Achieving Correctness, Minimizing Human Effort, Explaining Decisions. They also adopt Euzenat & Shvaiko's classification of ontology matching techniques into eight categories (string-based, language-based, constraint-based, graph-based, instance-based, model-based, formal resource-based, informal resource-based).
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The Kautz taxonomy and the KGA challenge mapping (Table 1) are directly relevant but need adaptation: the thesis focuses on indicator-level semantic matching rather than full KG/ontology alignment, and requires emphasis on multilingual and M&E-specific heterogeneity not covered here.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The paper identifies several NeSy architectures applicable to KGA: (1) loosely coupled approaches injecting formal knowledge into LLMs (K-BERT, instruction training/zero-shot via GPT-style models); (2) tightly coupled systems like Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) and Logical Neural Networks (LNN) that integrate symbolic constraints as loss regularizers or neuron-logic correspondences; (3) adversarial training frameworks integrating mapping and repair in a generator-discriminator architecture; (4) GNN-based structural learning combined with symbolic repair (e.g., Matcha-DL); (5) BERTMap as an LLM-based alignment system. Dominant state-of-the-art symbolic systems are AML/Matcha and LogMap. The paper argues tightly coupled NeSy (Type V in Kautz taxonomy, e.g., LTN) is most promising for jointly addressing mapping and logical repair.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The paper dedicates Section 4.5 to explainability challenges. It identifies: (1) post-hoc explanations using external formal knowledge (concept induction attaching formal knowledge to neural activations; path-based explanations generating logical reasoning paths across ontologies); (2) inherent explainability from tightly coupled NeSy systems like LNN and LTN which can generate explanations regardless of model complexity; (3) human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning (HLRL) for minimizing human effort in the repair phase, learning human preference functions to automate expert feedback, inspired by ChatGPT's RLHF approach. The paper proposes HLRL could be integrated as a discriminator in adversarial training or as a reward in RL frameworks.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: The paper primarily discusses biomedical ontology alignment (FMA, NCI, UMLS) and the OAEI evaluation tracks, particularly the Bio-ML track. Cross-lingual KGA is mentioned (GCN-align for cross-lingual alignment). No specific discussion of M&E, development indicators, or multilingual matching beyond cross-lingual entity alignment.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Lack of tightly coupled NeSy systems applied to KGA; scalability challenges for LNN and LTN on large-scale KGs; separation of mapping and repair processes causing redundancy; data inefficiency of current LLM-based approaches; limited explainability of deep learning KGA systems; no current fully integrated (Type VI) NeSy system exists; limited exploration of soft logical constraints for repair.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Integration of mapping and repair into adversarial training frameworks; use of LTN/LNN for soft repair with adjustable logical constraints; human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning for automated expert feedback; formal knowledge injection into LLMs (K-BERT, instruction training) for data-efficient alignment; combining GNNs with NeSy for structural learning; development of systems combining multiple NeSy approaches to surpass current state-of-the-art.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: Highly relevant: the paper's analysis of NeSy approaches for semantic matching across heterogeneous knowledge structures directly informs the thesis. The discussion of HITL via reinforcement learning, explainability through concept induction and path-based explanations, and tightly coupled NeSy (LTN/LNN) for soft logical repair are applicable to M&E indicator harmonization. However, the paper does not address multilingual indicator matching, M&E frameworks, or development contexts specifically. The adversarial training integration of mapping and repair and the soft constraint repair concept could be adapted for indicator equivalence validation.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This paper provides a structured analysis of how NeSy integration can address five key challenges in knowledge graph alignment (scalability, heterogeneity, correctness, human effort, explainability), mapping each to specific NeSy approaches using the Kautz taxonomy. It offers a theoretical framework and research roadmap directly relevant to the thesis, particularly its proposals for tightly coupled NeSy systems (LTN/LNN) for soft logical repair, HITL reinforcement learning for validation, and post-hoc explainability via concept induction and path-based reasoning over formal knowledge.

reference.title: Knowledge graphs as tools for explainable machine learning: A survey

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not explicitly specified; covers literature from early 1990s to ~2020

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: Three-dimensional analytical toolkit classifying knowledge-based explainable (KBX) systems along: (1) Knowledge (KG) variables: type (domain/factual/common-sense), semantics (TBox/ABox/both), selection (manual/automatic), reusability, number of KGs; (2) Model (ML) variables: input type (tabular/images/text/other), method (rule-based/tree-based/neural nets/other), task (classification/prediction/regression), integration mode (internal/external); (3) Explanation (XP) variables: form (raw/natural language/visual/multimodal), type (categorical/mechanistic/functional), interpretability (post-hoc/integrated/hybrid). Systems are further organised by application domain: rule-based ML, image recognition, recommender systems, NLP applications, predictive tasks.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The KG-type/semantics/integration dimensions and explanation form/type/interpretability dimensions are directly relevant for classifying NeSy explainability mechanisms in indicator matching. However, the taxonomy does not address semantic matching, multilingual contexts, or M&E-specific concerns, so adaptation is needed to incorporate these dimensions.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant architectures include CNNs, RNNs, GCNs, autoencoders, and rule-based/ILP systems integrated with knowledge graphs. Integration modes are either internal (KG embedded in training) or external (KG used post-hoc). Common-sense KGs (ConceptNet, WordNet) are used for classification tasks (image recognition, QA), while factual KGs (DBpedia, Freebase, Wikidata) are used for prediction and recommendation. Domain ontologies are used in earlier rule-based approaches. Most systems rely on manual selection of knowledge from graphs.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Explanations are categorised as categorical (explaining properties of results), mechanistic (explaining mechanisms causing results), or functional (explaining behaviour/end-goal). Forms include natural language, visual, raw text, and multimodal. Interpretability is either post-hoc or integrated. HITL is identified as an open challenge: the survey notes that reinforcement learning and humans-in-the-loop in KBX-systems remain largely unexplored, and that human assessment is needed for benchmarking what constitutes a good explanation. Some systems incorporate user feedback for improved performance.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Application domains covered: rule-based machine learning/data mining, image recognition, recommender systems, natural language applications (QA, reading comprehension, conversational AI, storytelling), and predictive/forecasting tasks (loan prediction, stock trends, flight delays, health social networks). Languages are not explicitly discussed; the focus is primarily on English-language systems and datasets. Multilingual contexts are not covered.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Knowledge graph maintenance (completeness, correctness, freshness); identity management across multiple KGs (the sameAs problem); automated knowledge extraction from graphs (still mostly manual); limited exploitation of TBox reasoning due to computational costs; scalability of reasoning over very large KGs; lack of benchmarks for evaluating explanation quality; limited understanding of human role in KBX-systems; knowledge graphs used as fact silos rather than for capturing meaning through causal/temporal/modal relationships; difficulty integrating prior knowledge in deep architectures.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Automated knowledge extraction heuristics for large-scale KGs; centralised authoritative hubs for KG quality; identity management services and guidelines; reinforcement learning and HITL integration in KBX-systems; development of benchmarks for explanation quality with human assessment; capturing meaning beyond facts through narratives, causal, and spatio-temporal relationships; human-centric AI where machines augment rather than replace human capabilities.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: Highly relevant: the survey identifies HITL in KBX-systems as largely unexplored, aligning with the thesis focus on interactive validation. The identity management challenge across multiple KGs directly relates to heterogeneous indicator matching. The gap in automated knowledge extraction from graphs is relevant to M&E harmonization where domain ontologies must be navigated. However, the survey does not address multilingual settings, M&E indicator harmonization, or semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators specifically.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: Provides a systematic analytical framework (KG × ML × XP variables) for classifying neuro-symbolic explainable systems that integrate knowledge graphs, directly usable as a reference taxonomy for the thesis. Identifies the critical open challenge of HITL integration in knowledge-based explainable systems and the need for automated knowledge extraction from heterogeneous KGs, both central concerns of the thesis on interactive, explainable semantic matching of M&E indicators.

reference.authors: Jan Portisch, Michael Hladik, Heiko Paulheim
reference.title: Background knowledge in ontology matching: A survey

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors propose three interrelated classification systems: (1) Background knowledge sources classified by target domain (domain-specific vs. general-purpose), structuredness (structured vs. unstructured), and type (lexical/taxonomical [mono/multilingual], factual databases, Semantic Web datasets [single/linked], pre-trained neural models [mono/multilingual], textual unstructured, non-textual unstructured); (2) Concept linking strategies classified into given links, direct label linking, fuzzy linking, and Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD); (3) Background knowledge exploitation approaches classified into factual queries, structure-based approaches, statistical/neural approaches, and logic-based approaches. Systems are positioned in a source/strategy matrix combining the source and exploitation classifications.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The classification of background knowledge sources (domain-specific vs. general-purpose, structured types including pre-trained neural models and Semantic Web datasets) and exploitation strategies (factual queries, structure-based, statistical/neural, logic-based) is highly relevant for classifying NeSy approaches to semantic matching. However, the taxonomy does not explicitly address neuro-symbolic integration patterns, explainability mechanisms, or HITL validation, which are central to the thesis. The source/strategy matrix can be adapted to frame how NeSy systems combine symbolic background knowledge with neural exploitation strategies.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The survey identifies that most ontology matching systems use factual queries on lexical/taxonomical resources (especially WordNet) as their primary exploitation strategy. Structure-based approaches are almost exclusively applied on WordNet. Logic-based approaches (reasoning on external ontologies) were envisioned early but did not gain traction. Neural/statistical exploitation approaches are recent (from 2018 onward) and include pre-trained language models (BERT, BioBERT, SBERT, Google Universal Sentence Encoder) and knowledge graph embeddings (RDF2Vec via KGvec2go, Word2Vec). Systems like VeeAlign use sentence encoders; KGMatcher uses BERT and Google language models; ALOD2Vec uses KG embeddings. The survey documents a resource/strategy matrix showing that neural exploitation of background knowledge is underexplored but promising. The combination of symbolic background knowledge with neural exploitation strategies is implicitly a neuro-symbolic pattern.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Not covered. The survey does not explicitly address explainability mechanisms or human-in-the-loop validation in ontology matching systems. The ontology matching correspondence definition optionally includes an explanation field, but the survey does not analyze how systems generate or present explanations.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: The survey identifies a strong biomedical domain bias (almost all domain-specific background knowledge sources are biomedical: UMLS, UBERON, DOID, MeSH, BioPortal). An English language bias is documented, with most evaluation datasets and background knowledge in English. Multilingual matching is underexplored: there is only one OAEI multilingual track (MultiFarm) with few participants, and multilingual approaches mostly use translation APIs with simple factual query strategies. The survey notes the absence of domain-specific multilingual resources and evaluation datasets from non-biomedical domains (e.g., business, enterprise). Languages covered by multilingual resources include those supported by translation APIs (Microsoft, Google, Yandex) and multilingual resources like BabelNet, HowNet (Chinese-English), and the Universal Knowledge Core.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: 1) Underexploration of neural/statistical exploitation strategies for background knowledge. 2) Logic-based reasoning on external knowledge sources has not gained traction despite early vision. 3) Strong biomedical domain bias; enterprise and business matching problems are barely represented. 4) English language bias in both evaluation datasets and background knowledge. 5) Multilingual matching is underdeveloped, relying mostly on translation API lookups limiting reproducibility. 6) Most concept linking uses naive direct string lookup; Word Sense Disambiguation is unexplored. 7) Manual background knowledge selection prevails; automated selection approaches are rare. 8) Unstructured resources, pre-trained neural models, general-purpose knowledge graphs, and linked data are underexplored as background knowledge.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: 1) Broader exploration of neural exploitation strategies including pre-trained language models and embeddings. 2) Development of evaluation datasets from non-biomedical domains. 3) Exploration of multilingual matching with advanced NLP models rather than simple translation API lookups. 4) Non-English rooted matching problems with non-English background knowledge. 5) Development and comparison of multiple linking/anchoring approaches. 6) Self-configuring matching systems that automatically select background knowledge. 7) Application of structural methods on multilingual and Semantic Web datasets beyond WordNet.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: Highly relevant. The survey documents the exact gap that the thesis addresses: the lack of neural exploitation strategies combined with symbolic background knowledge (i.e., NeSy approaches) for semantic matching. The identified biases toward biomedical domains and English monolinguality directly motivate the thesis's focus on multilingual M&E indicator harmonization. The absence of HITL/explainability discussion in ontology matching further supports the thesis's contribution. The classification of background knowledge sources and exploitation strategies provides a framework adaptable for characterizing NeSy-based semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This survey provides a comprehensive classification of background knowledge sources, concept linking strategies, and exploitation approaches in ontology matching (2004–2021), covering 341 publications. It documents that neural exploitation of symbolic background knowledge is an emerging but underexplored direction, identifies critical biases toward biomedical domains and English monolinguality, and reveals the absence of HITL and explainability considerations in current matching systems—all of which directly frame the thesis's research gap at the intersection of NeSy, multilingual semantic matching, and interactive validation for M&E indicator harmonization.

reference.authors: Cheng, K., Ahmed, N.K., Rossi, R.A., Willke, T., Sun, Y.
reference.title: Neural-Symbolic Methods for Knowledge Graph Reasoning: A Survey
reference.source: ACM Transactions on Knowledge Discovery from Data, 18(9), Article 225

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not explicitly specified; covers literature up to 2024

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors propose a taxonomy organized along two axes: (1) reasoning task type — KG completion (link prediction, complex query answering) vs. rule learning; and (2) method paradigm — pure symbolic methods (boolean logic inference, probabilistic FOL via MLN/PSL, ILP, association rule mining), pure neural methods (translation-based, bilinear, deep learning-based KG embeddings, RL-based path reasoning, representation learning-based FOL query answering), and neural-symbolic integration methods (embedding-based variational inference of MLN, PSL-based regularization for embeddings, differentiable ILP/neural theorem provers, matrix-based reasoning/neural logic programming, learning-based rule generation). A secondary classification organizes methods on a spectrum from rule-discovery-focused to KG-completion-focused (left-to-right) and from symbolic-dominant to neural-dominant (top-to-bottom).
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The taxonomy is comprehensive for KG reasoning but does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators, HITL validation, or multilingual aspects. The three-way split (symbolic / neural / neural-symbolic) and sub-categories of NeSy integration (variational inference, PSL regularization, differentiable ILP, matrix-based, learning-based) can be adapted to classify NeSy approaches relevant to indicator harmonization.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant NeSy architectures identified include: (1) Embedding-based variational inference of Markov Logic Networks (pLogicNet, ExpressGNN, pGAT); (2) PSL-based regularization of KG embeddings (KALE, RUGE, UKGE, FSL); (3) Differentiable ILP / Neural Theorem Provers (NTP, NTP 2.0, CTP, mILP); (4) Matrix-based neural logic programming (TensorLog, Neural-LP, DRUM, NLIL); (5) Learning-based rule learning (RNNLogic, RLogic, R5, NCRL); (6) RL-based multi-hop reasoning (DeepPath, MINERVA, MWalk, MultiHop, CURL); (7) Representation learning-based FOL query answering (GQE, Query2Box, BetaE, FuzzQE, GNN-QE). The survey highlights that neural-symbolic methods combine interpretability and generalizability of symbolic logic with scalability and correlation-capturing ability of neural networks.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The survey discusses interpretability as a key advantage of symbolic and neural-symbolic methods over pure neural approaches. Symbolic reasoning provides explicit traced reasoning processes and chains of evidence. Rule learning methods produce human-readable Horn rules with confidence scores. RL-based path reasoning produces interpretable multi-hop reasoning paths. The survey identifies 'Developing interactive reasoning systems' as an open future direction, mentioning dialogue systems, visual interaction platforms, meaningful explanations of reasoning outcomes, and collaborative exploration/refinement of KG knowledge by users — but no concrete HITL mechanism is reviewed in existing methods.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Application domains mentioned include search engines, healthcare (clinical decision-making, medical diagnosis, drug repurposing), smart cities, finance, law, and logistics. Standard KG benchmarks used include WN18, FB15K, FB15K-237, YAGO3-10, and Kinship. No multilingual aspects or M&E indicator contexts are discussed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Scalability-interpretability trade-off remains unresolved. Information loss from logical rules when sampling ground rules. Lack of effective action-filtering in RL-based methods. Computational inefficiency of differentiable ILP and matrix-based reasoning on large KGs. Neural logic programming cannot learn symbol embeddings. Most methods perform one-time rule injection rather than iterative embedding-rule interaction. Handling of noisy and uncertain data in KGs remains challenging.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Four future directions are highlighted: (1) Temporal reasoning over KGs; (2) Multi-modal reasoning integrating text, images, audio, video; (3) Developing interactive reasoning systems with HITL capabilities; (4) Combining KGs with Large Language Models to mitigate hallucination and improve factual grounding.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The survey does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators, multilingual settings, or M&E harmonization. However, the NeSy taxonomy and the identified gap in interactive reasoning systems with HITL are directly relevant to the thesis. The discussion of interpretable rule learning, logical rule-enhanced embeddings, and the need for collaborative knowledge refinement provides a theoretical grounding for NeSy-based indicator matching with human validation.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This survey provides a comprehensive reference taxonomy for classifying NeSy methods applied to knowledge graph reasoning, distinguishing five sub-categories of neural-symbolic integration. It confirms that interpretability and HITL interactive reasoning are recognized open challenges in the NeSy-KG field, supporting the thesis motivation. The systematic comparison of symbolic, neural, and neural-symbolic method capabilities (Table 1) offers a reusable analytical framework for evaluating NeSy approaches to semantic indicator matching.

reference.title: Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning: A Survey of Representation Learning, Rule-based Learning, and Neural-Symbolic Hybrid Methods
reference.source: 2025 IEEE 10th International Conference on Data Science in Cyberspace (DSC)

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: Three-paradigm taxonomy for Temporal Knowledge Graph reasoning: (1) Representation Learning (sub-divided into GNN-based, RNN/Transformer-based, Tensor Decomposition, Geometric Embedding); (2) Rule-based Reasoning Learning (sub-divided into Symbolic Rule Learning and Differentiable Rule Learning); (3) Fusion of Rule-based and Representation Learning (sub-divided into Early Fusion — symbolic constraints injected during embedding learning, Late Fusion — separate training then combined at inference, and Iterative Hybridization — alternating suggest-validate-revise cycles).
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The early/late/iterative fusion taxonomy for neuro-symbolic integration is useful for classifying NeSy methods in the thesis, but the survey is specific to temporal KG reasoning and does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators, requiring adaptation to the thesis domain.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant NeSy architectures identified include: Early Fusion (e.g., LLM-DR injecting rule constraints via LLMs and diffusion models into embeddings), Late Fusion (e.g., Neuro-Symbolic Temporal Point Processes with differentiable logic rule learning trained separately then combined), and Iterative Hybridization (e.g., TLINet with differentiable temporal logic reasoning modules). Underlying neural components include GNNs (TKGAT, TGN, HyGNet), RNN/Transformer hybrids (Re-Temp), tensor decomposition (TeLM, TComplEx), and geometric embeddings (BoxTE, ReTIN). Symbolic components include AMIE+, AnyBURL, DRUM, and RLvLR.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The survey identifies interpretability as a core challenge. Rule-based methods provide clear logical reasoning paths. Neuro-symbolic hybrid methods aim to balance neural performance with symbolic interpretability. Causal modeling integration (CSI, CognTKE dual-system theory) is highlighted as a future direction for transparency. Falsifiability-inspired frameworks generating verifiable hypotheses are proposed. No explicit HITL mechanisms are discussed.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Domains covered are political events (ICEWS18), global news (GDELT), cross-domain knowledge (Wikidata12k), and general facts (YAGO11k). No multilingual or M&E application contexts are discussed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Deep integration of causal modeling and interpretability; automated rule mining under sparse data conditions; multimodal and cross-domain knowledge fusion; synergistic utilization of LLMs; lack of falsifiability and verification in reasoning outputs.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Combine causal reasoning with neural-symbolic models; joint optimization of automated rule mining and embedding training; multimodal TKG construction with transfer/meta-learning; integration of LLMs and prompt learning into symbolic reasoning frameworks; design of reasoning models that generate testable hypotheses with verifiable evidence.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The survey focuses exclusively on temporal KG reasoning and does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators, M&E harmonization, HITL validation, or multilingual contexts. The early/late/iterative NeSy fusion taxonomy and the emphasis on combining interpretability with neural performance are conceptually relevant to the thesis but require significant adaptation to the indicator matching domain.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This survey provides a structured taxonomy of neuro-symbolic fusion modes (early, late, iterative hybridization) that can inform how NeSy integration patterns are classified in the thesis. Its analysis of the interpretability-performance trade-off in knowledge graph reasoning, and the identification of causal modeling and LLM integration as future directions, offer useful contextual framing, although the survey does not address semantic matching, HITL, or M&E indicator harmonization.

reference.title: Symbolic Logic Meets Machine Learning: A Brief Survey in Infinite Domains

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: Three strands organizing the logic-learning landscape: (1) Logic vs. Machine Learning – problems solvable by either logic-based or ML techniques, focusing on probabilistic inference via weighted model counting/integration; (2) Machine Learning for Logic – learning logical artifacts such as formulas and logic programs, using entailment-based scoring or likelihood-based scoring; (3) Logic for Machine Learning – logic as a meta-theory for ML providing tractability, correctness (verification, semantic loss), modularity (abstraction, probabilistic programming), and compositionality (semiring-based frameworks).
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The three-strand taxonomy (logic vs. ML, ML for logic, logic for ML) provides a useful conceptual framing for positioning NeSy integration patterns, but it does not directly map to the Kautz (2022) NeSy types nor to semantic matching or indicator harmonization tasks. It can be adapted to situate the thesis's NeSy architecture within the 'Logic for ML' strand (constraints, explainability, compositionality).

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The survey describes several integration patterns: neuro-symbolic probabilistic logic programming (e.g., DeepProbLog), weighted model counting/integration (WMC/WMI) as computational backends, knowledge compilation and circuits for tractable inference, semiring programming for composing heterogeneous computations, and probabilistic relational models (e.g., Markov Logic Networks). No single dominant architecture is identified; instead, a landscape of complementary approaches is presented.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Explainability is mentioned as a motivation for integrating logic with ML (Section 1). Abstraction is discussed as a mechanism that could help generate explanations by formally relating low-level data representations to high-level user understanding (Section 4, citing Gunning 2016 XAI and Sreedharan et al. 2018). No specific HITL mechanisms or interactive validation protocols are described.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Not covered. The survey is domain-agnostic and focuses on theoretical foundations. No specific application to multilingual settings, monitoring and evaluation, or indicator harmonization is discussed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: The survey notes that despite deep learning successes, model re-use, transferability, causal understanding, relational abstraction, explainability, and data efficiency remain open challenges requiring symbolic augmentation. It also notes the misconception that logic is limited to discrete domains, and that extensions to infinite (continuous and countably infinite) domains remain underexplored.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Recursive combination of learning and reasoning (strand 2 and 3 interleaved); formal realization of System 1 vs. System 2 processing; using abstraction as a general-purpose tool for reducing infinitary statements to finite computations; compositional AI systems interfacing learned modules with search and optimization via semiring frameworks.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The survey provides theoretical grounding for why logic-based constraints and symbolic artifacts are needed alongside neural learning, which is relevant to the thesis's NeSy approach. However, it does not address semantic matching, M&E indicator harmonization, multilingual settings, or HITL validation. Its value is in framing the general NeSy integration rationale and the role of explainability/abstraction, not in providing directly applicable methods.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This narrative review provides a theoretical framing for the integration of symbolic logic and machine learning, organized into three complementary strands. It supports the thesis rationale that symbolic constraints, abstraction, and compositionality are essential for explainable NeSy systems. It does not address semantic matching or M&E contexts but offers foundational vocabulary and conceptual scaffolding for positioning the thesis's NeSy architecture within the broader logic-learning landscape.

reference.authors: Kursuncu, Ugur; Gaur, Manas; Sheth, Amit
reference.title: Knowledge Infused Learning (K-IL): Towards Deep Incorporation of Knowledge in Deep Learning
reference.source: Proceedings of the AAAI 2020 Spring Symposium on Combining Machine Learning and Knowledge Engineering in Practice (AAAI-MAKE 2020)

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors propose a taxonomy of knowledge incorporation depth called 'Shades of Knowledge-Infused Learning': (1) Shallow infusion — knowledge used before or after learning (e.g., feature engineering, post-hoc validation); (2) Semi-deep infusion — knowledge injected at the attention/input level (e.g., K-BERT, ERNIE); (3) Deep infusion — knowledge infused within the hidden layers of neural networks via Knowledge-Aware Loss Function (K-LF) and Knowledge Modulation Function (K-MF). They also distinguish components: Seeded Sub-Knowledge Graph creation, Knowledge Embedding (Ke), Knowledge Infusion Layer (K-IL), and Differential Knowledge Engine.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The shallow/semi-deep/deep infusion taxonomy provides a useful lens for classifying how NeSy primary studies integrate symbolic knowledge into neural architectures. However, it needs adaptation because it does not address explainability mechanisms, HITL validation, or semantic matching tasks directly relevant to the thesis.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The paper identifies and reviews several NeSy-relevant architectures: knowledge-enriched NLMs (K-BERT, ERNIE, SensEmBERT), knowledge-based recurrent attention neural networks (KB-RANN), logic-rule-augmented DNNs (Hu et al. 2016), and the authors' own proposed Knowledge Infusion Layer (K-IL) that injects KG embeddings within hidden layers of LSTMs using KL-divergence-based differential knowledge and Hadamard modulation.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The paper argues that knowledge infusion provides explanatory insight into model decisions by making the role of domain knowledge traceable (information traceability). KGs serve as scaffolding for 'punctuating neural computing' to build explainable AI. However, no formal HITL validation loop is described; explainability is framed as a benefit of knowledge infusion rather than an evaluated mechanism.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Application domains discussed include mental health (Reddit posts classified to DSM-5 disorders using UMLS/ICD-10 KGs), online Islamist extremism detection on social media, conversational AI, lung cancer diagnosis from CT images, and cybersecurity. No multilingual or M&E indicator contexts are addressed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: The paper identifies the following gaps: (1) most existing approaches only incorporate knowledge shallowly (before/after learning), not within hidden layers; (2) optimal sub-knowledge graph selection and proximity (number of hops) remains an open problem; (3) merging latent and symbolic representations in different vector spaces is unsolved; (4) knowledge propagation through layers needs further investigation; (5) dependency on large datasets and bias in ML remain unresolved without knowledge infusion.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Deep infusion of KG knowledge within hidden layers of neural networks; dynamic evolution of Seeded Sub-KGs via differential knowledge engines; use of variational autoencoders for differential sub-KG extraction; broader deployment in healthcare and social science domains; building explainable AI through KG scaffolding.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The K-IL framework's concept of infusing structured domain knowledge into neural representations is relevant to the thesis goal of matching heterogeneous M&E indicators via NeSy methods. The shallow/semi-deep/deep taxonomy can help classify how indicator ontologies might be integrated into neural matching models. However, the paper does not address multilingual settings, HITL interactive validation, or semantic matching of indicators, leaving a clear gap that the thesis can address.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This position paper provides a theoretical framework (Knowledge-Infused Learning) and a depth taxonomy (shallow, semi-deep, deep infusion) for integrating knowledge graphs into neural network architectures. It offers foundational concepts—Knowledge-Aware Loss Function, Knowledge Modulation Function, and Differential Knowledge Engine—that can inform the design of NeSy architectures for explainable semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators in the thesis.

reference.authors: Meziane, L.; Abbaoui, W.; Abdellaoui, S.; El Bhiri, B.; Ziti, S.
reference.title: Narrative Review on Symbolic Approaches for Explainable Artificial Intelligence: Foundations, Challenges, and Perspectives

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: Four-category classification: (1) Logical approaches (formal logic, predicate logic, probabilistic logic); (2) Ontological approaches (ontologies for knowledge representation); (3) Expert systems (modern expert systems and their role in explainability); (4) Hybrid approaches (combining symbolic methods with deep learning, i.e., neuro-symbolic AI). Additionally, a comparative framework across Symbolic vs. Connectionist vs. Hybrid approaches along criteria: representation, reasoning, learning, explainability, scalability, uncertainty management, application areas, advantages, limits, and examples.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The four-category classification (logical, ontological, expert systems, hybrid) provides a useful high-level framing for symbolic AI components but lacks specificity for semantic matching tasks, NeSy integration depth (e.g., Kautz types), or indicator harmonization contexts. The comparative table (Symbolic vs. Connectionist vs. Hybrid) with explainability scores can be adapted as contextual framing for justifying hybrid NeSy choices.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The review identifies key neuro-symbolic architectures: DeepProbLog (merging probabilistic logic with deep learning), neural theorem provers (applying deep learning to formal reasoning), IBM's Logical Neural Networks (combining ontologies with neural models), and symbolic embeddings mapping symbolic concepts to neural-friendly representations (e.g., RAVEN). No specific architecture for semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators is discussed.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The review discusses explainability as a core advantage of symbolic AI: traceable decisions via logical reasoning, rule-based justifications, and ontological structuring. Interpretability scores are reported comparatively (symbolic: 92–98%, connectionist: 15–35%, hybrid: 75–90%). HITL is not explicitly discussed beyond a general reference to interactive machine learning for health informatics (Holzinger 2016). No specific HITL validation mechanisms are detailed.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Application domains covered: healthcare (medical diagnosis, clinical decision support, e.g., MYCIN, IBM Watson for Oncology), finance (fraud detection, regulatory compliance, e.g., ACTICO, MiFID II), industry (predictive maintenance, industrial control systems), law and ethics (regulated decision-making, e.g., ROSS Intelligence), cybersecurity (attack detection via multimodal learning), and autonomous vehicles. No multilingual contexts or M&E applications are discussed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Scalability of symbolic systems for large knowledge bases; rigidity in handling uncertainty (requiring extensions like fuzzy logic); high cost of manual knowledge engineering; lack of standardized benchmarks for explainability; challenges in integrating symbolic and neural components seamlessly.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Automated rule extraction from neural models; dynamic ontologies for evolving knowledge; hybrid architectures balancing scalability and complexity; automated knowledge acquisition; standardized explainability benchmarks; interdisciplinary collaboration bridging technical, ethical, and regulatory demands.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The review provides general background on symbolic AI for explainability and neuro-symbolic hybridization but does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators, M&E harmonization, multilingual settings, or HITL-driven indicator alignment. The identified gaps (scalability, automated knowledge engineering, dynamic ontologies) are tangentially relevant to the thesis but the review offers no direct contribution to the specific research context of ex-post M&E indicator harmonization via NeSy.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This narrative review provides broad contextual framing on symbolic AI foundations and their role in explainability, along with a comparative overview of symbolic, connectionist, and hybrid approaches. It identifies DeepProbLog and neural theorem provers as key neuro-symbolic architectures and highlights the need for dynamic ontologies and automated knowledge engineering—themes relevant as background motivation for the thesis's NeSy-based indicator harmonization approach.

reference.authors: Peng, Y.; Bathelt, F.; Gebler, R.; Gött, R.; Heidenreich, A.; Henke, E.; Kadioglu, D.; Lorenz, S.; Vengadeswaran, A.; Sedlmayr, M.
reference.title: Use of Metadata-Driven Approaches for Data Harmonization in the Medical Domain: Scoping Review

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors classify metadata-driven (MDD) approaches into: ontology-based (ontology of source/target applied in ETL/ELT), rule-based (transformation rules generated from source/target metadata), and other (UML-based, graphic-based, ad hoc formalisms-based, MDA-based, message-based, template-based, metadata-based without further specification). They also categorize thematic domains into 7 groups: medicine, data warehouse, big data, industry, geoinformatics, archaeology, military. Purposes are categorized as: automate data transformation, reuse ETL/ELT process, improve performance, develop generic ETL/ELT, develop ETL/ELT automatically, develop new ETL/ELT, and other.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The taxonomy of MDD approaches (ontology-based, rule-based, template-based, etc.) and their purposes is relevant to understanding how metadata and ontologies can drive semantic harmonization processes, but the review does not address NeSy integration, explainability, or HITL mechanisms, so the taxonomy would need substantial adaptation for the thesis context.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The review identifies ontology-based and rule-based approaches as the two most frequently used metadata-driven methods for ETL/ELT data harmonization. Ontology-based approaches use ontologies (e.g., in RDF/OWL) of source or target data models to establish generic mapping logic. Rule-based approaches use transformation rules stored in mapping sheets, CSV, YAML, or database tables. Some approaches combine ontology-based and rule-based methods. No NeSy architectures are described.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Not covered. The review does not discuss explainability mechanisms or human-in-the-loop validation.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Primary domains are medicine (n=9) and data warehouse (n=13), with additional domains including big data, industry, geoinformatics, archaeology, and military. The medical use cases focus on transforming EHR data (FHIR to OMOP CDM, i2b2, openEHR). The review mentions cross-country heterogeneity due to different languages (e.g., German vs English) and country-specific FHIR profiles, but does not address multilingual semantic matching explicitly.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Lack of comprehensive characterization and evaluation of MDD approaches to determine the most suitable ones for specific ETL/ELT purposes. Difficulty in determining an appropriate MDD approach and tool for generic FHIR-to-OMOP CDM transformation. Some publications used proprietary or non-transferable approaches limiting reproducibility.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Evaluation of the identified MDD approaches to determine the most appropriate ones and their integration into ETL/ELT processes, specifically for MII CDS FHIR to OMOP CDM transformation. Potential combination of multiple MDD approaches (e.g., ontology-based + rule-based) for comprehensive harmonization.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The review addresses data harmonization via ontology-based and rule-based metadata approaches, which is conceptually adjacent to semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators. However, it does not cover NeSy integration, explainability, HITL validation, or M&E indicator harmonization. The multilingual dimension is mentioned only as a source of heterogeneity (country-specific FHIR profiles) without proposing solutions. Relevance to the thesis is limited to providing contextual understanding of metadata-driven harmonization pipelines.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This scoping review provides a systematic inventory of metadata-driven approaches (ontology-based, rule-based, and others) for data harmonization in the medical domain, identifying their purposes and tools. It offers contextual background on how ontologies and transformation rules are used in ETL/ELT pipelines to address data heterogeneity, which is tangentially relevant to understanding the broader landscape of semantic harmonization, though it does not address NeSy, explainability, or M&E contexts.

reference.authors: Keber, M.; Grubišić, I.; Barešić, A.; Jović, A.
reference.title: A Review on Neuro-symbolic AI Improvements to Natural Language Processing
reference.source: MIPRO 2024, 47th MIPRO ICT and Electronics Convention, IEEE

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors classify NSAI methods along two axes: (1) Regularization type — how symbolic knowledge enters the DL model: rule-constrained model, modification of DL architecture, aggregation of inputs/hidden representations with symbolic KR, and pipeline; (2) Effect category — tangible benefits: data efficiency (DE), performance, explainable AI (XAI), and parameter efficiency (PE). They also map each method to Kautz types (Type 2, Type 3, Type 4, Type 5) and record NLP task, application area, symbolic KR source, learning approach, and optimization.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The regularization-type axis (rules, architecture modification, aggregation, pipeline) and effect categories (DE, performance, XAI, PE) are useful descriptive dimensions for characterizing NeSy integration in semantic matching. However, the taxonomy is NLP-task-centric and does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators or M&E contexts, requiring adaptation.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant NeSy architectures identified: (1) Rule-constrained models using Lagrangian dual, PSL, or teacher-student distillation over Bi-LSTMs, GRUs, and BERT; (2) DL architecture modifications injecting symbolic knowledge into multi-head attention (MHA) adjacency matrices via constituency/dependency trees, factual relations, or ontology-derived graphs (e.g., Sentic GCN-BERT, BioBERT+MDAtt); (3) Aggregation approaches creating multi-modal input representations using KG embeddings, GloVe, ConceptNet-Numberbatch, and GATs; (4) Pipeline approaches using prompt tuning with external KBs (ConceptNet, Wikipedia) or external API calls (Toolformer, PAL). Kautz types 2 and 4 are most frequent; Type 3 and Type 5 also appear.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Three XAI-related works are discussed: (1) MetaMap uses EBRO ontology-generated explanations evaluated by a human-in-the-loop for biomedical NER with active learning; (2) KERMIT uses a surrogate model with POS constituency importance to explain XLNet sentiment predictions; (3) GRU-NSR for Boeing manuals produces explainable outputs via manually created rules. The survey notes that LLMs still lack explainability, and human-centric AI requires fairness and trustworthiness.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Application domains: biomedical/healthcare (cancer registry, COVID-19 NER, drug-drug interaction RE), commerce, social texts, news, spoken language, user manuals (aeronautic maintenance), general NLP benchmarks. Languages mentioned in benchmarks: English, German (En-De MT). No specific multilingual or M&E contexts are addressed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: The survey notes that advantages of NSAI for NLP have received little attention from researchers. Most authors did not report model parameter counts, limiting PE evaluation. Explainability remains under-explored in LLM-based NSAI systems. RAG reduces hallucinations but risks of frozen model misuse remain.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Data-efficient NSAI models could be used in assisted annotation tools for various NLP tasks. Efficient fine-tuning, prompt tuning, and reasoning structures attempt to overcome the cost of training LLMs. The combination of symbolic structured information and connectionist models is presented as a sound alternative overcoming limitations of individual approaches.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The regularization taxonomy and effect categories provide a useful lens for describing how symbolic knowledge (ontologies, rules, KGs) can be integrated into neural models for semantic matching tasks. The HITL and XAI findings (especially MetaMap's active learning with ontology explanations) are relevant to interactive validation in M&E harmonization. However, the survey does not address semantic matching of heterogeneous indicators, multilingual settings beyond En-De MT, or M&E application contexts.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This survey provides a structured taxonomy of four regularization types (rules, architecture modification, aggregation, pipeline) and four effect categories (data efficiency, performance, XAI, parameter efficiency) for NeSy integration in NLP, with Kautz-type mappings. It documents concrete HITL and XAI mechanisms in NeSy NLP systems, and identifies data efficiency as the strongest benefit of NeSy approaches — all of which inform the architectural and explainability dimensions of the thesis's NeSy framework for M&E indicator harmonization.

reference.authors: Yuliang Li, Jinfeng Li, Yoshihiko Suhara, Jin Wang, Wataru Hirota, Wang-Chiew Tan
reference.title: Deep Entity Matching: Challenges and Opportunities
reference.source: Journal of Data and Information Quality (JDIQ), Volume 13, Issue 1

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not explicitly specified; references span approximately 2010–2020

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors organize deep EM solutions into two main families: (1) Non-Transformer-based methods (DeepER with Siamese LSTM, DeepMatcher with attention-based attribute alignment, MPM for multi-perspective matching, Seq2SeqMatcher for heterogeneous schema relaxation, HierMatcher with hierarchical token/attribute/entity alignment layers, CorDEL with contrastive symbolic token operations); (2) Pre-trained Transformer-based methods (BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT fine-tuned for sequence-pair classification). They further categorize optimization strategies: domain knowledge injection (span typing, span normalization), data augmentation (span-level, attribute-level, entity-level operators), and summarization (TF-IDF-based). Future directions are organized into blocking integration, training data reduction (active learning, transfer learning, data augmentation), matching different entity types, and explaining matching outcomes.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The taxonomy focuses on deep learning architectures for entity matching over structured/semi-structured records and does not address neuro-symbolic integration, ontological reasoning, or multilingual indicator harmonization. However, the categorization of explainability approaches (attention analysis, rule synthesis, model approximation) and the distinction between structural vs. semantic heterogeneity are relevant dimensions that can be adapted for the thesis framework.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant architectures include pre-trained Transformer LMs (BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT) fine-tuned for binary sequence-pair classification (Ditto), Siamese LSTM-based RNNs (DeepER), attention-based attribute alignment networks (DeepMatcher, Seq2SeqMatcher, HierMatcher), and contrastive token-set operations combined with word embeddings (CorDEL). Ditto serializes entity records with special [COL]/[VAL] tokens and injects domain knowledge via span typing tags. No explicit neuro-symbolic (logic-neural) integration is described; domain knowledge injection in Ditto is limited to tagging spans and normalization rather than symbolic reasoning.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The article identifies explainability of matching outcomes as a critical open challenge. It discusses: attention weight analysis for Transformer models (noting limitations and controversies around attention as explanation); rule synthesis from matching examples (SystemER); approximation of black-box models with interpretable surrogates (LIME, SHAP, shallow decision trees); global vs. local explanations. Active learning with human-in-the-loop annotation is discussed for reducing labeling costs (Kasai et al. 2019, Meduri et al. 2020). The article also discusses bias and fairness concerns in EM decisions.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Domains: product matching (Amazon-Google, Walmart-Amazon, Abt-Buy, WDC products), bibliographic records (DBLP-ACM, DBLP-Google), restaurant matching (Fodors-Zagats), music (iTunes-Amazon), company matching, job matching (discussed as future application). Languages: English only (all benchmarks are English). No multilingual experiments or discussion.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Explainability of deep EM models remains largely unsolved; attention-based explanations are controversial and limited to local examples. No benchmark datasets exist for matching different entity types or entities in different data formats. Steep training data requirements persist for deep learning EM. Combining blocking and matching in a unified framework is underexplored. Structural information from semi-structured or nested data is lost during serialization/flattening.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Synergistic integration of blocking and EM steps; unified framework combining active learning, transfer learning, and data augmentation to minimize labeling; generalizing EM to heterogeneous entity types and data formats (structured, semi-structured, unstructured); developing global and local explanation capabilities for deep EM; addressing bias and fairness in matching decisions; jointly learning structure and semantics of data.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The article highlights the need for explainable matching outcomes and human-in-the-loop validation, which directly relates to the thesis's HITL and explainability requirements. However, the article does not address neuro-symbolic integration with formal ontologies or logical reasoning, multilingual matching, or M&E indicator harmonization. The domain knowledge injection in Ditto (span typing) is a lightweight form of symbolic guidance but does not constitute full NeSy integration. The identified gap around matching heterogeneous entity types across different formats is relevant to the thesis's heterogeneous indicator matching problem.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This article provides a comprehensive overview of deep learning approaches to entity matching, establishing the state of the art with pre-trained language models (Ditto/RoBERTa). It identifies explainability of matching decisions and reduction of training data requirements as critical open challenges, and discusses HITL via active learning. While it does not address neuro-symbolic integration, ontological reasoning, or multilingual settings, it offers useful contextual framing for the thesis on how neural approaches handle structural and semantic heterogeneity in matching tasks and where their explainability limitations lie.

reference.title: Neural Networks for Entity Matching: A Survey
reference.source: ACM Transactions on Knowledge Discovery from Data (TKDD), Volume 15, Issue 3

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not explicitly stated; covers methods up to 2020

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: Four-category taxonomy based on two binary axes: (1) attribute-aligned vs. non-attribute-aligned comparison, and (2) independent vs. interdependent representation. Categories: Rigid Encoders (attribute-aligned, independent), Flexible Encoders (non-attribute-aligned, independent), Attribute Comparators (attribute-aligned, interdependent), Record Comparators (non-attribute-aligned, interdependent). The taxonomy also maps how each category relates to blocking and schema matching capabilities.
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The taxonomy focuses on entity matching architectures using neural networks and covers schema matching and blocking dimensions relevant to heterogeneous indicator matching. However, it does not address symbolic reasoning, ontological constraints, or explainability mechanisms central to NeSy approaches, so adaptation is needed to incorporate knowledge-guided and explainability dimensions.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: Dominant architectures include RNN-based models (BiLSTM, BiGRU) with attention mechanisms, CNN-based models, and transformer-based models (BERT). Key methods: DeepMatcher (attribute comparator with cross-attention), Ditto (transformer-based record comparator using pretrained language models), DeepER (flexible encoder with LSH blocking), Seq2SeqMatcher (record comparator with joint schema matching), Hi-EM (attribute comparator with character-to-record hierarchical representation). Transformer-based networks (Ditto, Brunner & Stockinger) represent current state-of-the-art. Transfer learning from pretrained language models is increasingly used.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: Explainability is identified as a major challenge. The survey notes that as entity matching steps coalesce into large neural networks, fewer intermediate checkpoints are available for inspection. Attention alignment scores are mentioned as one existing technique, but the authors state the field is far from comprehensive debugging capabilities. Human-in-the-loop is discussed as crucial for entity matching in practice, with references to crowdsourcing and active learning (e.g., Kasai et al. using deep networks with active learning), but the survey finds neural networks are not yet tightly coupled to HITL techniques. Running time in interactive settings is flagged as a challenge.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Domains covered in evaluation datasets include publications (DBLP-ACM, DBLP-Scholar), products (Amazon-Google, Abt-Buy, Walmart-Amazon), restaurants (Fodors-Zagats), music (iTunes-Amazon), beer, and electronics. Historical datasets involve census/medical records (not publicly available). Multilingual aspects are not a primary focus, though pretrained embeddings are noted as available for different languages, and fastText subword embeddings help with domain-specific and OOV words. No specific multilingual entity matching experiments are reported.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: Explainability and debugging of deep neural networks for entity matching remain underdeveloped. No end-to-end method combines schema matching, blocking, record pair comparison, and classification jointly. Lack of diverse open datasets (especially industrial domains). No publicly available pretrained models specifically for entity matching. Running time challenges limit interactive/HITL use. High training data requirements for deep learning methods.
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: Development of end-to-end approaches integrating schema matching and blocking with comparison and classification (flexible encoder architecture). More open and diverse datasets across domains. Standardized benchmarks beyond precision/recall (including blocking, transfer learning, active learning evaluation). Publicly available pretrained entity matching models. Attribute-type-specific pretrained models that can be mixed and matched. Transformer-based networks for pretrained schema matching.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: Highly relevant: the survey's identification of gaps in schema matching integration, explainability, and HITL coupling directly parallels the thesis need for explainable semantic matching of heterogeneous M&E indicators. The taxonomy's distinction between attribute-aligned and non-attribute-aligned comparison is pertinent to matching indicators with incompatible schemas. However, the survey does not address symbolic knowledge integration, ontological reasoning, or multilingual indicator harmonization, which are central to the thesis NeSy focus.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy of deep neural network architectures for entity matching organized by representation independence and attribute alignment, directly applicable to characterizing the neural component of NeSy semantic matching systems. Identifies explainability and HITL integration as key open challenges in neural entity matching, reinforcing the thesis motivation for neuro-symbolic approaches that can provide interpretable matching rationales for heterogeneous indicator harmonization.

reference.authors: Diaz-de-Arcaya, J.; Garcia-Perez, A.; Bonilla, L.; Miñón, R.; Torre-Bastida, A.I.
reference.title: Data Harmonization as a Keystone for Data Spaces: Challenges, Techniques, and Future Trends

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not specified (no explicit temporal bounds given)

section_2_taxonomy.key_categories: The authors organize findings around four topics: (1) Challenges of data harmonization in data spaces (legal framework, privacy, disparate standards, data silos, heterogeneity, interoperability, scalability, availability, different technologies, security, sustainability); (2) Stages of the data harmonization lifecycle (Preparatory Stage, Data Collection and Initial Analysis, Standardization and Data Modeling, Data Transformation and Integration, Validation and Quality Assurance, Implementation and Continuous Improvement); (3) AI techniques for automated data harmonization (NLP, GANs, XAI, Latent Diffusion Models, Reinforcement Learning, Common Data Model); (4) Standards, frameworks, and maturity models for data harmonization in IoT data spaces (FAIR principles, GAIA-X Semantic Data Link, EU regulations).
section_2_taxonomy.adoption_rationale: The six-stage data harmonization lifecycle and the AI technique categorization provide a useful contextual framework, but the review does not address NeSy integration patterns, semantic matching of indicators, or HITL validation specifically. The lifecycle stages and challenge taxonomy can be adapted to frame the data harmonization context in the thesis.

section_3_findings_by_rq.rq1_architectures: The review identifies NLP, GANs, Explainable AI, Latent Diffusion Models, Reinforcement Learning, and Common Data Models as AI techniques used for automated data harmonization. It briefly mentions knowledge graphs with symbolic AI for interpretable hybrid AI systems in medicine (reference [23]). No explicit NeSy architectures or integration typologies are discussed.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq2_explainability_hitl: The review mentions Explainable AI (XAI) as a technique to ensure harmonization does not compromise interpretability, help identify biases or inconsistencies, and support transparent and equitable results. It references XAI in radiomics for selecting robust features. No explicit HITL validation mechanisms are described.
section_3_findings_by_rq.rq3_domains_languages: Primary domains covered include healthcare (electronic health records, medical imaging, clinical narratives, cancer registries), agriculture, industrial production, smart cities, environmental/chemical risk assessment, and IoT-enabled data spaces. No multilingual aspects are discussed.

section_4_gaps_trends.gaps_identified: The review identifies interoperability, data silos, disparate standards and nomenclatures, legal and regulatory complexity, data heterogeneity, privacy, scalability, and sustainability as persistent challenges. It notes the lack of standardized harmonization frameworks within data spaces (only one paper found on GAIA-X Semantic Data Link).
section_4_gaps_trends.future_directions: The authors foresee the implementation of a data harmonization framework that streamlines the generation of quality data products for various domains oriented to a data economy. They highlight the need for advanced frameworks for interoperability and the integration of AI techniques with regulatory compliance.
section_4_gaps_trends.link_to_thesis_gap: The review confirms that data harmonization across heterogeneous sources remains a major challenge, and identifies NLP and XAI as relevant techniques, but does not address NeSy integration, semantic matching of M&E indicators, HITL validation loops, or multilingual harmonization. This gap aligns directly with the thesis research space.

section_5_synthesis.main_contribution_to_thesis: This review provides a comprehensive contextual framing of data harmonization challenges, lifecycle stages, and AI techniques applicable to heterogeneous data integration. Its six-stage lifecycle model and challenge taxonomy can contextualize the thesis problem, while its identification of NLP and XAI as key techniques for automated harmonization supports the relevance of NeSy approaches. However, it does not address neuro-symbolic integration, semantic indicator matching, or HITL mechanisms, confirming the thesis gap.

reference.title: Large Language Models for Semantic Join: A Comprehensive Survey

section_1_scope.period_covered: Not explicitly specified; references span approximately 2016–2025
